title: Building LLM Apps That Survive Production
excerpt: Treat the model like any other unreliable dependency: timeouts, retries, structured outputs, and a graceful path back to humans.
content:
# Building LLM Apps That Survive Production

Most LLM apps I've seen die in production the same way: the demo works, the first week in production works, and then one day the model hallucinates, the retry loop runs away, and the bill lands.

Here's the playbook I use now.

## 1. Treat the LLM as an unreliable dependency

Your LLM call should look like any other network call:

- **Timeouts**: hard upper bound, e.g. 5 seconds. The model doesn't get to decide when to answer.
- **Retries**: bounded, with exponential backoff, and a circuit breaker.
- **Structured outputs**: never trust free-form text. Force JSON, validate it, and reject if it doesn't parse.

## 2. Confidence thresholds beat vibes

The model tells you how sure it is. Use it. Below 0.7, escalate to a human with full context. Above 0.95, auto-execute. In between, queue for review.

## 3. Log everything

Every prompt, every completion, every token count, every cost. You'll thank yourself the first time something goes wrong.

## 4. Have a kill switch

A single env var that bypasses the model entirely and falls back to a hardcoded behavior. Test it in staging every deploy.

## 5. Cost is a feature

Track cost per request. Surface it on a dashboard. Set alerts. The model that costs $0.001 per call looks cheap until you do 10M of them.

---

LLMs are powerful, but they're not magic. The teams that win are the ones that ship them with the same engineering rigor they'd ship a database with.

title: ITSM Is a Software Problem
excerpt: The teams that treat incident management as a software problem — with code, data, and feedback loops — outperform the ones that treat it as a process problem.
content:
# ITSM Is a Software Problem

There's a popular narrative that ITSM is about process: CAB meetings, RACI charts, ITIL books. That's true at the 30,000-foot view.

At ground level, ITSM is a software problem.

## What does that mean?

It means:

- Your incident playbook should live in code, not in a Word doc
- Your change requests should be API objects, not email threads
- Your post-mortems should be queryable, not PDFs in a folder
- Your SLA dashboards should be real-time, not monthly reports

## The teams that win

The teams that win at ITSM treat it like an engineering system. They:

1. **Automate the boring parts.** Ticket triage, on-call rotation, status pages — all automated.
2. **Measure everything.** MTTR, change failure rate, incident frequency, mean time to detect.
3. **Close the loop.** Every incident produces a corrective action. Every corrective action has an owner. Every action has a due date.
4. **Make data visible.** Dashboards that leadership actually opens.

## What changes

When you treat ITSM as a software problem, several things happen:

- Your on-call rotations get fair (no more "same three people")
- Your incidents get shorter (because the runbook is right there)
- Your changes get safer (because the risk is scored, not gut-feel)
- Your executives get honest (because the data is visible)

---

ITSM isn't a process you follow. It's a system you build.

title: Why I Built This Portfolio (and What I Learned)
excerpt: Notes from shipping a portfolio that's also a CMS, an AI demo, and a stress test of my own taste.
content:
# Why I Built This Portfolio (and What I Learned)

Most engineer portfolios are static. This one isn't. It has:

- A real admin panel
- An AI assistant that knows my work
- A blog with a CMS
- Live analytics
- A 3D scene that doesn't tank performance on a 2019 MacBook

## The goal

When a recruiter lands here, they should feel that this is the work of someone who sweats the details. When a developer lands here, they should want to read the source.

## What I learned

1. **The admin panel is the product.** The home page is marketing; the admin is the engine. Building the admin first kept the rest honest.
2. **3D is a tax, not a feature.** Every Three.js line is a performance line. Be ruthless about what you render.
3. **The AI assistant is mostly retrieval.** Most questions about me are answered by my own content. RAG + a tight prompt is enough.
4. **Performance budgets aren't optional.** Lighthouse 100 is achievable if you decide it matters before you start.

## What's next

I'll keep writing here about the engineering behind the systems I build. If you want to follow along, the RSS feed is at `/feed.xml`.
